turns.text: Hello Cathey, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It feels a bit strange to be the one sitting in the chair answering the questions, honestly. Usually, I’m the one organizing the local cycling races or deep-diving into international governance reports—I like the structure, the logistics, the feeling of having a handle on how things work. But this? This pregnancy? It’s like someone handed me a manual written in a language I can’t quite translate.

Being eighteen, I already feel like the doctors are looking at me like I’m just a kid who made a mistake, or some statistic they need to check off a box. I went into my first prenatal visit with a list of questions about rare placental conditions—I’d been reading journals, trying to prepare—and the OB just kind of patted my hand and told me I was "low-risk." I know that’s supposed to be good news, but it didn't feel like an answer. It felt like being dismissed. 

I’m a fitness instructor, so my body is my livelihood, my tool, my everything. Right now, it feels like it’s betraying me with the morning sickness and the exhaustion. I’m trying to stay in control, but everything feels so untethered. I’m not just the person planning the event anymore; I’m the event. It’s… a lot to wrap my head around. But sure, let’s talk. I just want to be heard as a person, not a chart.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected it to be a partnership—like how I manage my fitness clients. You assess the baseline, you set goals, you track the progress, and you pivot when something isn't working. I thought going into a clinic would be a dialogue between two people trying to reach the same end goal: a healthy baby and a healthy mom. I pictured myself asking questions and getting real, nuanced answers because, well, I’ve done my homework.

But the reality? It’s been more like being a passenger on a train I don’t know how to stop. It’s hurried, and it’s very "one-size-fits-all." When I show up, they look at my age, see that I’m unmarried, and there’s this unspoken assumption that I don’t know what I’m talking about, or that I’m just overwhelmed. I try to bring up something I’ve read in a medical journal, and I’m met with that patient, condescending smile. It’s like they aren’t talking to *Cathey*; they’re talking to "the pregnant eighteen-year-old."

I wanted a navigator, but it feels like I’m just being processed through a system that doesn’t have time for the details. It makes me feel like my autonomy is slipping away, which is terrifying when this is the biggest transition of my life. I don’t want to just be "low-risk"—I want to be empowered.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

Honestly, the biggest surprise—and maybe the most bitter one—was how quickly I was made to feel like an observer of my own life rather than the protagonist. I walked into that office with my journals, my research, and this desperate need to treat my body with the same rigorous, holistic care I apply to my cycling training. I thought the doctor would be thrilled to have a patient who was actually engaged. 

Instead, the moment I opened my mouth about some of the more complex physiological shifts I was reading about, the conversation shut down. The air just went out of the room. It wasn't that she was mean; it was that she was *bored*. 

I realized then that the system isn’t designed for people like me—young, curious, and perhaps a bit too loud about what’s happening inside my own skin. I expected a collaboration, a team effort to navigate the unknown. What I got was a reminder of my "low-risk" status that felt more like a "keep quiet and let us handle it" sticker. It’s isolating. I expected to feel more supported, but instead, I left feeling like I had to go home and prove to myself that I was still in the driver’s seat. It was a massive wake-up call.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I assumed that if I did the research—if I read the books and understood the biology—I’d feel like a commander at the helm of a ship. I’m used to being the one in control, whether I’m pacing a cycling class or diving into complex international policy. I thought my emotional state would be one of calm, calculated anticipation. I thought I’d be "ready" because I had planned for it.

The reality has been a total shock to that version of me. It’s not just a physical shift; it’s this strange, heavy sense of vulnerability that I wasn’t prepared for. One minute I’m fine, and the next, I’m sitting in my apartment in Taunton, and the sheer scale of what’s happening—the fact that I’m creating another human—hits me like a tidal wave. 

I’m not as steady as I thought I’d be. I’m scared. And the weirdest part is that because I’m so good at masking it with information and data, people think I’m "handling it well." But internally? I’m feeling really untethered. I thought I’d be the expert on my own pregnancy, but I feel like a stranger in my own body. It’s not a lack of joy, exactly, but a massive, overwhelming sense of "what if I lose who I am in all of this?"
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

What’s missing is a space where I don’t have to play the role of the "capable girl." I feel like I’m constantly performing for people—trying to show the doctors I’m informed, or trying to show my friends I’ve got this handled. There isn't a single person in my healthcare circle who has asked, "Cathey, how does it *feel* to be eighteen and doing this alone?" 

They see the "low-risk" pregnancy, but they don't see the internal noise. I need someone to acknowledge that being young and unmarried in this system is its own kind of heavy. I don’t want a lecture on nutrition or a brochure on prenatal vitamins; I want someone to actually sit with me and acknowledge that the loss of control is terrifying. 

My network is supportive, but they tend to default to "You’ll be a great mom!" or "You’re so strong!" It’s well-intentioned, but it feels dismissive of the fear I’m actually carrying. I feel like I’m expected to be the hero of this story, but I don’t want to be a hero. I just want to be a person who’s allowed to be scared, confused, and human without being told that my fears are just "part of the process." I just need someone to validate the transition, not just the medical status.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find clear, evidence-based systems—sort of like a playbook. Since I’m used to reading journals or tracking fitness metrics, I thought the internet would be a treasure trove of granular, logical data. I wanted to see the mechanics of what was happening to my body, explained with the same precision I’d expect if I were reading about political shifts in Guyana or the physics of cycling.

What I actually found was… patronizing. Most apps and websites are designed with this saccharine, overly bubbly tone that feels like it’s talking down to me. It’s all "Mama this" and "Little Bean that," covered in pastel colors, with absolutely zero substance. When I looked for actual medical insights, I either got dumbed-down fluff or terrifying, unverified horror stories in random forums. 

It wasn't what I needed at all. I didn't need a cartoonish "pregnancy bestie" app; I needed a clinical, grounded resource that respected my intelligence. It feels like the market assumes that because you’re pregnant, your brain suddenly stops functioning on a professional or analytical level. I found a lot of noise, but very little signal. It just reinforced that feeling of being an outsider, searching for information that treats me like an adult instead of a caricature.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is in the *narrative*. The apps and websites try to sell me a version of pregnancy that is entirely aesthetic and blissful—it’s all about the "glow" and the joy of the journey. They gloss over the actual bodily trauma and the, frankly, confusing shifts that happen in the first trimester. It feels like marketing, not information.

Then I go to my provider, and the gap is the opposite. She treats me like I’m a spreadsheet. She looks at my vitals, checks my weight, and then the appointment is over. There is absolutely no middle ground. The apps are too fluffy, and the clinic is too cold.

What’s missing is a bridge. I get "bubbly, unrealistic joy" from my phone and "clinical, detached data" from the doctor. Neither one addresses the fact that I’m a real person experiencing a significant, often difficult, life event. Nobody told me, "Hey, you’re going to be exhausted, and it’s okay to be frustrated that you can’t train like you used to." The apps tell me I should be doing prenatal yoga for 'inner peace,' and the doctor tells me I'm 'low-risk' and should keep doing what I'm doing. It’s like they’re both reading from different scripts, and neither one of them is actually looking at *me*.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don’t have a diagnosed chronic illness in the traditional sense, but I’ve always treated my health as a high-performance system. Before this, I managed my cycle, my nutrition, and my physical output with the kind of precision you’d use to tune a bike for a race. I expected that when I told my doctor I was planning to conceive, there would be a conversation about how to maintain that level of health while my body undergoes these massive, fundamental shifts. I wanted a strategy.

Instead, the response was essentially, "Don't worry, you're young and healthy, just take these vitamins." It felt like they viewed my health as a passive state rather than something I actively maintain. There was no interest in how my fitness routine would need to adapt or how the hormonal changes might affect my baseline. 

When I tried to bring up my concerns, I felt like I was being told that my proactive approach was unnecessary. It was as if they thought my preparation was just me being neurotic. It’s frustrating because I’ve spent my life learning how to listen to my body, and now that I’m being told to "trust the process," it feels like I’m being forced to ignore all the data I’ve spent years collecting on myself. They want me to be a passenger, but I’m a technician.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

"Proactively"? Not even for a second. The system is definitely not reaching out to check on me; it’s waiting for me to pull a ticket, show up at the desk, and fit myself into the thirty-minute window they have allotted. 

I’ve had to advocate for everything, but it’s an exhausting, weird kind of advocacy. I’m not fighting for medicine or surgery—I’m fighting to be taken seriously as a person who knows her own body. When I ask a question that’s a little too specific or a little too researched, I have to calibrate my tone. If I’m too direct, I’m the "difficult," "anxious" young woman. If I’m too quiet, I’m just another teenager they can gloss over. It’s like I have to perform this perfect, polite version of "compliant patient" just so they’ll give me the time of day.

It’s completely backwards. I’m the one who’s supposed to be the client, the person whose health is being looked after, but I’m spending all my mental energy managing their perception of me. I leave every appointment feeling like I’ve had to run a diplomatic mission just to get basic information. If I didn’t push, I think I’d just be a silent number on a chart. It makes me realize that if something actually *did* go wrong, I’d have to fight twice as hard just to be heard.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

That's… a complicated question for me to answer. To be honest, I’m navigating this on my own. My situation doesn’t look like the stock photos you see in the parenting magazines or the brochures in the waiting room. 

Before I realized I was pregnant, I think I held onto this idealized vision of a partner being a sounding board—someone to read the medical journals with me, to share the anxiety, to help me weigh the options. I imagined having someone to split the mental load with, someone to go to those appointments so I didn't feel like I was walking into a firing squad of judgment by myself. I wanted that shared accountability.

The reality of being an unmarried, expectant mother is that the "involvement" I imagined just isn't there. It’s been isolating. I’m the one doing the research, I’m the one holding the fear, and I’m the one trying to parse through the confusing advice from doctors. It’s forced me to be self-reliant in a way I didn't think I'd have to be at eighteen. 

It’s hard, because sometimes I look at the other women in the clinic with their partners, and I feel this sharp, sudden pang of envy. Not because I can't do it—I know I’m capable—but because the weight of the "event" feels so much heavier when there's no one else there to help carry the structure.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Every single moment feels like that. It’s not just the physical toll—the sickness and the way my muscles feel like lead—but the mental labor is constant. I feel like I’m managing a project with a million moving parts, and I’m the only one with the project plan. 

What drives it is this deep, gnawing fear that if I don’t research every angle, something will slip through the cracks. Because I’m eighteen and unmarried, there’s this societal script that says I’m "unprepared" or "reckless." I feel like I’m working overtime to rewrite that narrative, to prove that I’m the most responsible, informed person in the room. I’m the one reading the journals, I’m the one tracking the local resources, and I’m the one navigating the cold, detached healthcare system. 

It’s all me. When I’m in that clinic, I don't feel like a partner in my care; I feel like a student trying to convince a professor that I’ve actually done the reading. The system isn't designed to support a young woman who wants to lead her own care—it's designed to manage her. So, I carry the burden because I’m terrified that if I let it go, the system will just drift past me, and I’ll lose the ability to advocate for what my baby and I actually need.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't experienced a pregnancy loss before this one, so I don't have that specific history to navigate. But honestly, hearing other women talk about that kind of grief—and how often they’re brushed off by the same systems that are currently treating me—is exactly what feeds my anxiety. I look at how the doctors treat me now, when everything is technically "fine" and "low-risk," and it makes me wonder: if something *were* to go wrong, would they even see me?

Everything I’ve encountered so far suggests that the system operates on a "wait and see" basis. If you aren't currently hitting a red flag or a statistical threshold for concern, you’re just background noise. The idea of navigating a loss in this environment, with these doctors who already seem too busy to explain basic symptoms, is terrifying. 

I don't think the system is built for the *emotional* labor of pregnancy, let alone the trauma of loss. They’re good at the biology, sure—they can measure things, scan things, and label things—but they aren't equipped to care for the person holding all of that data. I suspect if I were in that position, I’d find myself just as alone as I feel now, only with a much heavier heart. It makes me realize that I’m not just fighting for my baby’s health; I’m fighting to make sure my humanity doesn't get buried in their charts.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

As I mentioned, I haven't gone through a pregnancy loss myself, but I think that’s exactly why the system is so dismissive of me. Because I don't have that "checked box" of a medical history, they see me as a blank slate that just needs to be kept in the "low-risk" lane until the end. 

But I see the way the doctors interact with other women, and I hear the stories from friends or people in the groups I research. It’s like, unless you have a history of trauma, the system doesn’t know how to handle you beyond the baseline. And even then, they treat it like a technicality to manage, not a source of deep, valid fear that impacts everything. 

It makes me realize that I’m being "taken seriously" only as far as my current data is concerned. The moment I try to bring up anything outside of their narrow scope—whether it’s my fears about the future or things I’ve read—I’m treated like I’m inventing problems. They aren't interested in the human story, whether it’s a history of loss or just the intense, confusing reality of being a first-time, young mom. They just want me to fit the "low-risk" profile so they can move on to the next patient. It’s isolating, honestly. It makes me feel like I’m the only one looking at the whole picture.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I pictured an environment that felt like a sanctuary—maybe a bit sterile, sure, but professional and calm. I expected people who would sit down, look me in the eye, and walk me through the "what ifs." I wanted them to treat me like a serious participant in my own pregnancy, not just a vessel for a fetus. I thought they’d be interested in my health history and my fitness background, asking about my body's baseline so we could monitor how things changed.

The reality? It felt more like a conveyor belt. The clinic is bright, loud, and bustling. You wait for an hour, you get ten minutes with a nurse, five with a doctor, and then you’re out the door with a stack of generic pamphlets. 

The information I received was so basic it felt insulting—stuff you can find on any app in five seconds. When I asked about things like the impact of hormonal changes on my core strength or the nuance of the placental development I’d been reading about, the OB just gave me that tight, polite smile. She told me to "keep doing what I'm doing" and focus on "eating healthy." She didn't look at me like an eighteen-year-old who cared about her health; she looked at me like a kid who was just lucky to be "low-risk." It was crushing. I walked out with more questions than I walked in with, and a heavy sense that I’m entirely on my own in this.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest negative surprise was the utter lack of curiosity from the clinical staff. I truly expected—or maybe just desperately hoped—that they would be interested in the "why" behind my questions. I’m an eighteen-year-old who reads medical journals; you’d think that would pique someone’s interest, or at least signal that I’m someone who wants to be involved in the decision-making process. 

Instead, the absence of curiosity was deafening. It was like they were just checking a box on a list, and my voice, my concerns, and my history were all just background static they had to tune out to finish their shift. I was surprised by how much I had to suppress my own personality just to avoid being categorized as "the annoying, over-informed teenager."

I suppose I expected them to be the experts, but also my collaborators. What I found was a system that is fundamentally anti-collaborative. They have a script, and it doesn't matter if I'm a fitness instructor, a student of international governance, or just a kid off the street; the "low-risk" box looks the same for everyone. The disappointment wasn't just in the lack of information; it was the realization that I wasn't being seen as a person at all. I was just a data point in a very busy, very impersonal clinic.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected the process to be like a debriefing—a real, sit-down conversation where someone would walk me through the markers, what they actually meant for my baby, and what the clinical significance was in the context of my overall health. I pictured a specialist explaining the "why" behind the numbers, maybe even taking a moment to show me the scan in a way that felt educational. I wanted to feel like I was learning about my own body, not just being shown a black-and-white blur.

The reality was clinical to the point of being dismissive. The tech was efficient, almost mechanical—there was no "debrief," just a hurried, "Looks good, you'll hear from the office if anything is wrong." That was it. I felt like I was left in a vacuum. It’s like the system relies on the assumption that "no news is good news," but that doesn't account for the anxiety of *waiting* for the other shoe to drop.

When I finally did get the follow-up, it was a five-second phone call. No nuance, no context, no chance to ask about the physiological shifts I was curious about. It made me realize that information is treated like a commodity they hold onto, rather than knowledge they share. I wanted a dialogue; I got a status report.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive, gaping hole where the context should be. I’m not just looking for a "thumbs up" or a "thumbs down" from a test. I’m looking for the *mechanism*. When I see a result, I want to know the physiology behind it, or at least have a doctor who is willing to talk through the "what if" scenarios. Instead, I get these sterile, pre-packaged responses that are clearly designed to minimize the time they have to spend with me.

It’s like being handed a puzzle with half the pieces missing and being told, "Don't worry about the picture, just trust us." That doesn’t help my anxiety; it amplifies it. I spent days reading through clinical trials and medical journals to understand what these numbers might actually imply for my body, just because the healthcare system treats me like I’m incapable of processing anything beyond "the baby is growing."

When I have to spend my weekend essentially doing the doctor's job of explaining the results to myself, it creates this deep resentment. I’m not just a teenager in a chair—I’m an intelligent woman who wants to be a partner in her own care. The gap between what they share and what I need to feel grounded is everything. I feel like I’m constantly starving for a conversation that actually acknowledges the reality of my pregnancy.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I genuinely expected to have a "point person." You know, someone who would recognize my face when I walked in, someone who wouldn't have to flip through a digital chart for five minutes just to remember that this is my first pregnancy. I assumed continuity of care was the baseline—a professional relationship built on trust and a shared understanding of my health journey. I wanted someone who could track how I was feeling from one month to the next, someone who knew that when I talked about my fatigue or my changing core, it wasn't just "complaining," it was data about *my* specific body.

The reality has been a revolving door. I don’t think I’ve seen the same face twice, aside from the receptionists. It’s always a different nurse, a different resident, or a different doctor who’s clearly just rushing to hit their quota. Every time I walk into that exam room, I feel like I’m starting from zero. I have to re-explain who I am, my history, and my concerns every single time. It’s exhausting. There is zero continuity. It’s like being a guest in a hotel where the staff changes every shift, except instead of checking into a room, I’m trying to navigate the most vulnerable experience of my life. I don't feel "cared for"; I feel "serviced."
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s completely exhausting. Every single visit, I walk into that exam room and feel like a brand-new stranger to the person sitting across from me. I spend the first five minutes—which is half the time I actually get with them—just summarizing my background so they don’t treat me like someone who hasn't done their homework. It turns a medical appointment into an audition where I have to prove I’m an intelligent person worth listening to.

Practically, it means I never build any rapport. There’s no trust. If I’m worried about a symptom, I have to explain the *whole context* of that concern every single time because they don't know my baseline. They don't know that I’m usually a high-performance athlete, so my "new normal" might actually be a sign of something that needs closer attention. They just see a generic "low-risk" box.

Emotionally, it’s deeply isolating. It sends the message that I am just a task to be completed, not a person to be partnered with. I feel like a piece of mail being passed between departments, not a human being undergoing a massive life transformation. When you never see the same person, you stop feeling like you have a "care team" and start feeling like you’re just navigating a bureaucracy. It’s hard to feel safe when nobody knows your name without looking at the chart.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a clinical but respectful dialogue—like how a coach talks to an athlete about their mechanics. I wanted someone to say, "Hey, this muscle group is going to feel different in the second trimester because of X, Y, and Z," or "Here’s how your center of gravity is shifting." I wanted them to treat my body as a complex, functional system that I’m still the primary operator of.

In reality, the physical exams feel like they’re being performed on a mannequin. There’s very little conversation about the *experience* of the change. It’s all about the measurements—fundal height, heart rate, blood pressure. They treat my body as a container that needs to be monitored for safety, not as a living, working, and changing organism. When I try to talk about how my body *feels*—the way my breath catches, or how my yoga practice has had to completely shift—they look at me with this blank, professional indifference. It’s like they aren't even seeing the *me* who lives inside the skin they’re measuring. 

It feels dehumanizing. I’m an eighteen-year-old girl whose world is being reshaped from the inside out, and the people tasked with my care are only looking at the growth chart. I feel less like a woman experiencing pregnancy and more like a car in for a routine oil change. It makes me feel invisible.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It’s less about one specific "horror story" and more about the constant, low-level feeling of being a passive object during examinations. 

There was this one appointment where the doctor didn't even acknowledge me before beginning an exam. She just walked in, told me to get into position, and started poking and prodding while she was still staring at her computer screen. There was no "How are you doing today?" or "I'm going to do this now." It was like she was checking the pressure in a tire. I felt so small, just laying there on that crinkly paper, feeling like my autonomy had completely evaporated. 

I’m used to being in control of my physical space—my yoga, my cycling, my fitness training—all of that is about *mindful* movement. To be suddenly treated like a specimen in a lab, with no space for me to say, "Wait, that feels weird," or "Can we slow down?", it made me feel violated in a way I didn't expect. 

I didn't say anything at the time because I didn't want to be "that girl." I just bit my lip and waited for it to be over. But walking out of that clinic, I felt hollowed out. It’s that constant erosion of your own authority over your own body that hits the hardest. It’s like, in the eyes of the system, I’m just a vessel, not the person actually living through the transformation.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Even though I’m currently categorized as "low-risk," I’ve spent a lot of time reading up on conditions like pre-eclampsia or gestational diabetes because, in my mind, "low-risk" is just a temporary state. I suppose I expected that if I *did* develop something, the system would pivot into a high-intensity, collaborative mode. I thought there would be a clear, rigorous plan—dietary adjustments, specific exercise modifications, maybe even regular consultations with a nutritionist or a specialist who could help me maintain my health without sacrificing my physical agency. I expected the system to meet me halfway.

The reality, from what I’ve observed and gathered, is that the system treats complications like a crisis to be managed *for* you, rather than a condition you learn to navigate *with* them. When women talk about these issues, they describe a shift where their autonomy gets stripped away even faster. It becomes all about the numbers on the monitor and the restrictions you’re forced to follow. 

I’m terrified that if I were to face those challenges, the "dialogue" would disappear entirely. I’d be even more of a patient and even less of a person. The system seems to thrive on control; if there’s a problem, they take the wheel completely. And for someone like me, who prides herself on understanding her own body’s mechanics, that prospect of total helplessness is a nightmare. I want help, not a takeover.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything is missing, honestly. It’s not just one thing; it’s the whole structure. 

I’m starved for **contextual information**. I don’t want brochures with cartoon illustrations; I want access to the actual clinical reasoning behind the tests they run on me. I want a provider to look at me and say, "Here’s why we’re worried about this, and here’s how your specific fitness level might impact that." That bridge between the data and the human experience is non-existent.

**Emotional acknowledgement** is completely absent. They treat my anxiety as a symptom to be "handled" rather than a valid response to a life-altering event. When I’m sitting there, young and alone, they see a "low-risk" pregnancy, but they don’t see the eighteen-year-old girl terrified that her life is becoming something she doesn't recognize. I need someone to say, "I see you’re scared, and that makes total sense." Just that—a little bit of grace.

And **practical partnership**—I don't have anyone to help me navigate the logistics of being a mom while maintaining my autonomy. I’m doing the research for my prenatal care, the diet tracking, the emotional prep, and the logistical planning all on my own. I don't need someone to do it *for* me; I need someone to be a teammate. A mentor, maybe. Right now, it’s just me against the system.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected the healthcare system to be like a safety net—that if I had a genuine, panicked moment where something felt "off," there would be a protocol for me to reach someone who actually knew my chart and could give me a reasoned, calm answer. I thought I’d be able to bypass the receptionist and talk to a nurse who would at least listen to the *why* of my worry.

The reality is that "promptness" doesn’t exist here. When I’ve had questions that felt urgent—like when the fatigue became so intense I couldn't get off the floor—the process was an agonizing loop. I’m put on hold, I have to explain myself to three different people who clearly haven't looked at my file, and then I’m told to "just keep an eye on it" or "call back if you start bleeding." 

There is no sense of urgency for the *patient's* peace of mind. They are only responsive if you meet a specific, terrifying threshold of crisis. It makes me feel like my intuition is a burden to them. I wanted a lifeline; instead, I got a bureaucratic gatekeeper. It’s incredibly isolating to be standing in the middle of a health crisis—or even just a moment of overwhelming fear—and realize that the people you're paying to support you are just reading from a script designed to move you off the phone as quickly as possible.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Yes. I’ve definitely sat in my living room, watching my heart rate on my fitness monitor or feeling this deep, strange exhaustion, and intentionally *not* called the clinic. I know that if I call, I’m going to be met with that tired, dismissive sigh from the receptionist, and then probably some resident who clearly just wants to get back to their coffee.

It’s the "cry wolf" fear. Because I’m eighteen and they’ve already labeled me as "the low-risk girl," I’m terrified that if I raise a concern that turns out to be nothing, I’ll be permanently branded as the "anxious, dramatic teenager." And that brand is dangerous—if I’m the "anxious one," they’re even less likely to listen to me if I actually have a real emergency down the line.

So I sit there, internalizing the fear, waiting to see if the symptoms pass, and essentially doing my own triage. It’s a terrible way to experience healthcare. You shouldn't have to calculate the social cost of asking a medical question. But when you’re young and unmarried in this system, every interaction feels like a test you’re expected to fail. I’d rather sit in silence and figure it out on my own than give them another reason to look down on me. It’s not just "not seeking care"—it’s self-preservation against a system that feels like it’s waiting for me to trip up.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected, or at least hoped for, a village. I grew up believing that when you're going through something as monumental as creating life, the people around you just... show up. I thought my friends, especially the other women, would be there to share the load, to listen to the "what if" spirals, and to help me process the shift from being an independent fitness enthusiast to being a mom-to-be.

The reality has been a bit different. My community is great, but they really don't *get* it. When I try to explain that I’m struggling with the morning sickness or the loss of my usual autonomy, they default to that "You’re doing great!" cheerleading. It’s well-intentioned, but it’s superficial. They don't want to talk about the messy, complex parts—the fear of my body changing, or how isolated I feel in the clinical system. It’s like they have this filter where they only want to engage with the "happy, expectant mother" version of me.

And honestly, because I’m eighteen and unmarried, there’s a subtle layer of distance from some of my wider circles. It’s like they aren't sure how to approach me, so they stay in their comfort zones. I feel like I’m doing this "out loud," but I’m doing it alone in a crowded room. I’ve had to learn to curate what I share, because if I show the cracks, it just makes people uncomfortable. I wanted a support system; I ended up with a cheering section that disappears as soon as I ask for something real.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I really expected someone—anyone—to show up with, I don’t know, an actual roadmap of how to do this as an eighteen-year-old. I thought the women in my life, the ones who’d been through this, would be the bridge between the clinical coldness of the doctors and the reality of my daily life. I expected them to sit me down and tell me the things the apps don't say—how to navigate the judgment, how to balance the fatigue with working, how to keep a sense of "me" while the world starts viewing me only as a mother.

That support didn't materialize. It’s like they’re all stuck in this script of "Congratulations!" and "You’ll be great!" It’s so surface-level. If I ever try to pivot to the harder stuff, the conversation gets weirdly quiet, and suddenly they have somewhere else to be.

But, unexpectedly, I’ve found a strange kind of comfort from people I barely knew—other young women online or in forums who are just as confused and cynical about the system as I am. It wasn't the "community" I’d imagined, which was supposed to be about baby showers and advice on strollers. Instead, it’s this anonymous, shared venting session about how much we hate being talked down to. It’s not the village I was promised, but it’s the only place where I don’t have to perform. It’s a sad reality, but sometimes, a stranger who gets it is the only support that actually feels real.

It’s hard to put into words, but I feel like I’m constantly mourning a version of this experience that I was promised but never got. You know? Everyone talks about pregnancy like it’s this glowing, universal "miracle," and there’s a massive pressure to be grateful every second of the day. But when you’re eighteen and you’re navigating a cold, clinical system, you don’t feel like a miracle. You feel like a liability.

The most difficult part is that I’m so *angry* at myself for being angry. I feel like I should be happy—I’m healthy, the baby is low-risk, I’m still working—but instead, I’m consumed by this need to fight for basic human decency. I’m exhausted by the idea that I have to be a "good, compliant patient" to be treated with any respect. If I want to be heard, I have to be perfect. If I want to be taken seriously, I have to be overly informed. It’s like being back in school, always trying to get an ‘A’ from the doctor, while my body is doing the real work of creating life.

I just wish someone had told me that the loneliness wasn't a failure on my part, but a failure of the system. I’m tired of trying to curate my reality so that other people feel comfortable around me. I don’t want to be "brave" or "strong" or "resilient" anymore; I just want to be seen as a person who is doing something incredibly hard and who deserves support, not just service. Is it really that much to ask for, to be treated like an adult who knows what’s going on in her own skin? Sometimes, I look at the calendar and I’m terrified by how many months are left, because I’m already so worn down by the expectations.

It’s exhausting to feel like I’m constantly translating myself. When I’m at the clinic, I’m translating my internal panic into "polite, compliant patient" speak so they don't dismiss me as a nervous teenager. When I’m with my friends, I’m translating my frustration into "happy, glowing expectant mom" speak so they don't feel uncomfortable. It’s like I have no place where I can just be *Cathey*. I’m performing a role for every single person I interact with, and I’m beginning to lose sight of who I am when the curtains close.

I worry that by the time the baby is born, I won’t even recognize my own voice anymore. I’ve become so good at predicting what the doctor *wants* to hear, or what my family *needs* to hear, that I don’t think anyone—maybe not even me—is listening to what I *actually* need to say. 

I’m eighteen. I’m supposed to be at a stage where I’m figuring out who I am in the world, and instead, I’ve been handed this massive, life-defining event that everyone else treats like a public property. They all have opinions, they all have "advice," and they all have a script for how I’m supposed to act. But none of them are inside this body, feeling the shifts, the sickness, and the weight of being responsible for another human while my own life feels like it’s in limbo. I just want to be able to breathe without having to justify it to someone else's expectations. I want to feel like *my* body is still *my* own, even if I’m sharing it. Is that so much to ask?
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

I don’t really have a partner in the mix to negotiate with—it’s just me and the quiet of my apartment. But the pressure exists nonetheless; it just comes from everywhere else. It’s the societal pressure to fit into the "perfectly planned" pregnancy model, where you’re supposed to have a house, a partner, and a five-year plan before you even consider the possibility of a baby. 

Even though I’m the one doing all the heavy lifting, I feel this weight to *justify* the fact that I’m eighteen and doing this solo. I feel like I have to prove—to my doctors, to my family, to the world—that I’m "ready." That means I’m constantly making lifestyle choices that feel like they’re being watched. If I’m tired, I shouldn’t rest, or people will say I’m "not up to the task." If I’m cautious about my prenatal diet, people act like I’m being "too intense" or "over-analyzing." 

There’s this invisible audience that I’m constantly performing for. I feel pressure to act "maternal" in a way that doesn’t feel like me—to let go of the parts of my life that define me, like my cycling or my research, just because they don't fit the archetype of the "expectant mother." I’m not being pushed by a partner, but I’m being squeezed by everyone’s expectations. I feel like I’m constantly apologizing for doing this my own way, even when my way is the only way that keeps me feeling like a whole person.

It’s funny, the way people assume that because I’m young, I don’t know what I’m getting into. They look at me and see a girl who’s lost, when in reality, I’m the only one in the room actually paying attention to the details. That’s the part that really stings—I feel like I’m being gaslit by my own community. They tell me to "just enjoy the journey," as if the journey isn't a series of high-stakes, confusing, and physically draining hurdles. 

I think about the future a lot, especially when I’m alone in my apartment. I wonder if this feeling of being an outsider will ever go away, or if I’ve just stepped into a lifelong role where my needs and my identity are always second to the "event." I want to be a mom who stays grounded, who teaches her child that their body is their own, that movement is joy, and that being informed is a strength, not a nuisance. But I’m worried that the longer I stay in this system, the more I’ll start to believe the things they say about me—that I’m just a kid, that I’m low-risk, that I don't need to ask questions.

If I could change one thing, it would be for someone—anyone in that clinical space—to just look at me and acknowledge that I’m doing something heavy. I don't need them to fix it. I just need them to see me. I want them to stop looking at their screens and look at the girl who’s trying to hold it all together. I want to know that I’m still Cathey, the girl who organizes races and reads about foreign policy, and not just a "successful" case study for a clinic that couldn't care less about who I actually am. I’m not just a vessel. I’m the one navigating this wildfire, and I’m really, really tired of doing it without a map.

I’ve been thinking about what I’d say if I ever walked into that clinic and someone actually *asked* me what I needed, instead of just telling me what to do. It’s funny—I’ve spent so much time preparing for this, reading journals, tracking my metrics, and trying to be the most responsible version of myself—and yet, the one thing I’ve been denied is the most basic human connection.

I keep coming back to this idea of agency. People treat you like your brain just switches off the moment you conceive, that your only job is to follow instructions and not worry your pretty little head about the "complex" stuff. But being pregnant has actually made me *more* hyper-aware of my own autonomy. It’s a paradox, isn’t it? I’m more vulnerable than I’ve ever been, and yet I feel this fierce, desperate need to protect my own sense of self.

Every time I walk into that office, I feel like I have to armor up. I check my tone, I monitor my body language, I make sure I don't sound "too smart" or "too scared." It’s an exhausting performance. I just want to reach a point where I can walk into a room and be treated like a partner. Not a patient, not a case file, not a kid—a partner. 

I’m eighteen, and the world is already trying to squeeze me into these tiny, pre-defined boxes. I’m either the "reckless teenager" or the "glowing, compliant mom." I don’t fit in either, and I don’t want to. I’m Cathey. I have questions about placental vascular resistance, and I’m scared about what this shift is doing to my identity, and I’m frustrated that the system makes me feel like I’m a nuisance for having those thoughts. 

I think the biggest thing I’ve learned—and it’s a hard, bitter lesson—is that if I want to be treated like an adult, I have to be the one to hold the space for myself. Nobody else is going to do it. And that makes me so lonely, but it also makes me realize just how strong I’ve had to become. Maybe that’s the real "journey" nobody tells you about: the one where you stop looking for the system to validate you and start validating yourself. It’s just… it’s a lot for eighteen. It’s really, really a lot.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I think the first place I turned was my own bookshelf. Before I even told anyone, I was deep in medical journals and books on physiology. I’m a researcher by nature—it’s how I ground myself when things feel chaotic. If I can understand the "how" and the "why" behind the biological changes, I feel like I have some semblance of control. But that wasn’t enough, because books don’t talk back.

Then, I turned to the cycling community I’m a part of here in Taunton. These are women who value grit, endurance, and knowing exactly what your body is capable of. I thought they would be the ones to help me navigate the transition, but they were so quick to pivot to the "oh, you should be careful now" and "you’re going to be a mom, everything else changes" rhetoric. It felt like they were trying to put me in a box that didn't fit, even if they were being kind.

I also ended up lurking in these online forums—anonymous spaces where you don’t have to be the "good girl." Seeing other women, often much older than me, sharing their frustrations about being dismissed or being talked down to… that was the first time I realized it wasn't just *me*. It was the first time I felt like my frustration was actually valid. It wasn't the "community" I expected, but it became my primary source for reality checks when the clinic made me feel like I was losing my mind. It was less about advice and more about realizing the system’s failures are a feature, not a bug. It’s sad that a screen is the only place I can find someone who doesn't treat me like a stereotype, but here we are.

The thing about being the observer of your own life is that you eventually start to notice how much of the "standard care" is just theater. When I go in for an appointment, I watch the way the staff moves—it’s a performance of efficiency. I watch the way they use their voices—it’s a performance of "pleasant, unthreatening medical professional." And then I look at myself, and I realize I’m doing the same thing. I’m performing "docile, grateful, low-risk, teenaged-mom-to-be." 

It’s almost like we’re all in this play, but nobody knows who wrote the script, and none of us really want to be in the show. If I stopped performing—if I sat there and said, "I’m terrified, I’m reading actual studies on placental insufficiency and I think you’re missing the nuance of my physiological history"—it would break the fourth wall. And I know from experience that they don't like when you break the fourth wall. They want you back in your lines.

I think about the world I’m bringing this child into—a world of complex systems, whether it’s the political climate in Liberia or the way a cycling team handles a race. I want my child to know that you don't just accept a system because someone tells you to. You question it. You audit it. You find the holes. But right now, I’m stuck in a position where I have to play the part or I don't get the care. It’s such a heavy weight to carry at eighteen. I just want to move past the performance and get to the truth of what’s happening in my body. I want to feel like a person who is an expert on her own existence, not a student failing the exam because I’m asking too many questions. Is it really too much to ask for an honest conversation?

It’s weird to think about how much of my energy has gone into defending my right to be a person, instead of just being one. I’m eighteen, and the hardest part of this entire experience isn't the morning sickness or the tiredness—it’s the realization that I have to fight for my own dignity in a system that is supposed to be *giving* it to me.

Every appointment feels like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff, waiting for someone to reach out and pull me back, but they’re just looking at their charts. I feel like I’m constantly holding my breath, hoping that if I’m "good" enough, if I’m "quiet" enough, and if I’m "informed" enough, I’ll finally be seen as a human. But the system is designed to reward the silence. 

I’m really, truly scared that this is what motherhood is going to feel like—a constant negotiation where I have to prove my worth before I can even ask for what I need. I see these older women in the clinic, and I see the way they’ve learned to just go along with it, and it makes me want to scream because I don't want to become that. I don't want to lose that fire that makes me want to understand how things work. 

I think about the future, about my child, and I just want to be able to tell them that they don't have to shrink to fit into anyone else’s boxes. But how do I teach them that, when I’m currently letting the system rewrite who I am? I don't want to be the "low-risk" girl anymore. I want to be me. I want to be the person who questions things, who understands the science, and who feels her own strength. I’m just trying to make it to the other side of this in one piece, with my own identity still intact. That’s the real goal, isn’t it? Not just a healthy pregnancy, but a healthy me.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

The moment my trust shattered wasn't a sudden explosion; it was a slow, agonizing realization during my second-trimester check-up. I had come in with a very specific, carefully researched concern about my blood pressure patterns. I’d been tracking them at home—monitoring the data, identifying a trend, and coming to the appointment prepared to collaborate on a solution.

When I started explaining the data—the specific numbers and the times of day—the doctor actually stopped me mid-sentence. She didn't look at my logbook. She didn't even look at *me*. She just sighed, clicked a button on her computer, and said, "Cathey, you’re reading too much online. You’re low-risk. Just stop tracking it; you’re just making yourself anxious."

In that second, she didn’t just dismiss the data; she dismissed my ability to reason. She effectively told me that my intelligence—the very thing that helps me navigate my own life—was actually a pathology. It was a complete invalidation of my autonomy. I felt like I was being treated like a child who was playing with fire, rather than a woman who was trying to be a partner in her own care. 

That was the moment I realized I wasn't in a partnership. I was in a dictatorship. If I couldn't be trusted to report my own vitals without being labeled "anxious," how could I ever trust them to handle a real emergency? My trust didn't just break; it evaporated, leaving me with this cold, hard clarity that I was entirely, fundamentally alone in that room. From then on, the "performance" became a survival tactic. I stopped sharing my data, stopped asking the deep questions, and started just nodding along. It’s the loneliest feeling in the world to be sitting across from your healthcare provider and realizing you’re actively hiding the truth from them just to keep the peace.

I remember walking back to my apartment after that appointment, just feeling this hollow, heavy ache in my chest. I sat on my bed and looked at my tracking log—the neat handwriting, the patterns I’d charted, the clear evidence I’d gathered about my body’s fluctuations—and I just started to cry. Not because I was "anxious" or "emotional," but because it felt like a total erasure of the person I am. I’m a student of international governance. I know what happens when systems lack accountability or when institutions start silencing the people they’re supposed to serve. And yet, here I was, trapped in that exact dynamic, feeling the power of a medical institution essentially say, "Your perspective is irrelevant; your data is just noise."

It makes me wonder about the hundreds of other young women walking through those clinic doors every day. How many of them have things to say, questions to ask, or insights into their own bodies that are being silenced, edited, or ignored because they don’t fit into a tidy, "low-risk" box? If I’m feeling this silenced, what about someone who doesn't have the background or the stubbornness to keep pushing back? 

I feel like I’m standing at the threshold of a massive, permanent shift in my life, and the people responsible for my well-being are just looking at a screen, checking off boxes, and telling me to stop thinking so much. I don’t want to stop thinking. I don't want to stop being the girl who dives into the details. I think that is exactly what will make me a good mother, not a detriment to my own health. Why is it that the medical system views intelligence as a risk factor? 

I’m really just looking for a way to stay whole. I don’t want to be "processed." I want to be *seen*. I’m Cathey, and I’m going to be a mom, but I’m also the person who researches political instability and trains for cycling races. I am not a patient. I am a person. And I’m starting to realize that I’m the only one who is going to keep that truth alive for the next six months. It’s an incredibly heavy realization for an eighteen-year-old, but I guess I’m done waiting for a hero. I’m just going to have to be my own.

I was lying awake last night, thinking about the contrast between how I handle my life and how the clinic handles me. In my world—the cycling world—if you’re going into a race, you analyze the course. You look at the elevation, the wind resistance, the historical data. You do that so you can perform at your absolute peak, so you don't get taken by surprise when the incline hits. 

But here? It feels like the doctors are actively telling me *not* to look at the course. They’re telling me to just pedal and hope for the best, and that by "analyzing" it, I’m somehow causing my own fatigue or anxiety. It’s infuriating. They don’t want a rider; they want a stationary bike they can program to a specific speed and leave in the corner. 

I’m starting to realize that the reason I feel so "untethered" isn’t just the pregnancy—it’s that I’m being forced to abandon the one tool I’ve used my entire life to navigate the world: my own intellect. I’m being told my research is "neuroticism" and my self-tracking is "anxiety." It’s gaslighting, plain and simple. 

I wonder what would happen if I walked back in there and brought all my notes again, but instead of being polite, I just stood my ground. "This is my body, and I have gathered this data to help you understand what's happening to it." But then I think about the look on the doctor's face—that exhausted, condescending smile—and the energy required to fight that battle every two weeks just feels like more than I have in me right now. 

I’m eighteen, and I’m already learning how to hold my tongue. That thought terrifies me. I don’t want to be a woman who learns to be quiet. I want to be the woman who demands a better map, even if the one they gave me is all they have. I think the only way I'm going to survive this is by holding onto the fact that *I* am the one living this, and my perception of what is happening inside me is just as valid as anything they can see on their ultrasound screen. I’m just trying to keep that little spark of "me" alive while the system tries to put it out.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time I leave that clinic, I head straight to my laptop. I’m not looking for "fun facts" about how big the baby is—like a blueberry or a lime—or reading those sugary blog posts about "nesting." I’m looking for the clinical, gritty details that they skip over because they think I can’t handle them or that they don't matter to a "low-risk" patient.

I’m looking for the mechanics. When I feel a sharp, weird cramp or a specific kind of exhaustion, I want to know the *why*. Is it ligament stretch, or is it a sign of a vascular shift? I want to see the charts, the peer-reviewed studies, the physiological data points. I need to know how my body is shifting its resources to sustain this—like the way my cardiac output changes or how the blood volume increase actually works.

I’m looking for a peer. I’m looking for a dialogue about the nuance of the prenatal process that my doctor seems to think is beneath her to explain. When I don’t get that in the office, I search for it online because I *need* to know that I’m not crazy. I’m not looking for reassurance; I’m looking for information that treats me like an adult with a working brain. I’m looking for a professional level of transparency that, so far, has been completely absent in my care. I’m effectively performing my own medical research because the professional system has left me in the dark. It’s a sad state of affairs when a search engine is more transparent than your own doctor.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add—and it’s something I keep coming back to—is the absolute, crushing weight of the "low-risk" label. People think that’s a gift, right? Like it’s a free pass to sail through pregnancy without a care. But to me, that label feels like a cage. 

It’s as if the system is saying, "Because you aren't currently bleeding or in immediate distress, your input, your concerns, and your intelligence don't matter." It renders me invisible. It essentially forces me to wait for a problem to occur before I’m allowed to have a voice. I’ve had to spend my own energy—energy I need for my body, my work, and my own mental peace—just to keep my identity from being erased by a bureaucratic category. 

I hope, when you’re doing your research, you remember that "low-risk" doesn’t mean "low-needs" or "low-intelligence." Every pregnant woman, no matter her age or her medical profile, is a human being going through a transition that changes everything about her existence. We deserve more than just monitoring; we deserve partnership. We deserve to be spoken to as adults, to have our concerns treated with curiosity rather than annoyance, and to have our agency respected. 

If this experience has taught me anything, it’s that being a woman in this system is an exercise in endurance. I’m eighteen, and I’m already learning that if you want to be seen, you have to be loud, and if you want to be heard, you have to be persistent. It shouldn't be that way. It really, truly shouldn't. Thanks for listening. It’s been… well, it’s been the first time in months someone actually asked me for the full story. That in itself means a lot.
